schools.summary: The new tables and the renewal of the law: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first: and I will write upon these tables the words that were in the first tables, which thou brakest' — God commanding Moses to hew two new stone tables (like the first, which Moses broke, Exodus 32:19), on which God would again write the words of the law (the new tables, the law renewed after the breach, the difference, the first tables God's own work, Exodus 32:16, these hewn by Moses, yet God again to write); God again writing the law (the same words, the unchanging law, vv.28, the law restored after the breach) — the renewal (the covenant and law renewed, the broken covenant restored by grace, the unchanging law, the same words, the moral law unaltered); 'which thou brakest' (the reminder of the breach, the first tables broken, the covenant broken by the calf, the providence, the law renewed after the apostasy, the figure of grace restoring the broken covenant, the patience of God renewing his word to the people who had broken it) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first: and I will write upon these tables the words that were in the first tables, which thou brakest
the new tables and the renewal of the law
the law renewed after the breach of the calf (Exodus 32:16, 19)
the broken covenant restored by grace, the unchanging law

schools.summary: The command for Moses to come up the mount: 'and be ready in the morning, and come up in the morning unto mount Sinai, and present thyself there to me in the top of the mount' — God commanding Moses to be ready and come up Sinai in the morning, to present himself to God on the top of the mount (the command, Moses to ascend again, the second time, to receive the renewed law, the meeting, Moses to present himself before God); Moses to come up the mount (the second ascent, to receive the law again) — the ascent (the mediator going up); 'present thyself there to me in the top of the mount' (Moses presenting himself before God, alone, vv.3, the meeting, the renewed communion, the receiving of the law and the proclamation of the Name, vv.5-7, the providence, Moses going up to meet God, the renewal of the covenant) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And be ready in the morning, and come up in the morning unto mount Sinai, and present thyself there to me in the top of the mount
the command for Moses to come up the mount
Moses called up the second time to receive the renewed law (vv.5-7)
the renewed meeting and communion with God

schools.summary: The mount kept clear for the meeting: 'and no man shall come up with thee, neither let any man be seen throughout all the mount; neither let the flocks nor herds feed before that mount' — no man (not even Joshua, as before, Exodus 24:13) to come up with Moses, nor be seen on the mount, nor were the flocks to feed before it (the seclusion, Moses alone with God on the mount, the holy mount kept clear, the reverence, the bounds set, the mount sacred, as before, Exodus 19:12-13); Moses alone (no man with him, the mediator alone with God) — the solitude (Moses the sole mediator, admitted to God's presence, the figure of Christ the one Mediator); 'neither let the flocks nor herds feed before that mount' (the holy mount kept clear, no man or beast, the bounds set, Exodus 19:12-13, the reverence, the sacredness of the meeting, the holiness guarded, the providence, Moses alone with God, the renewed meeting in reverence) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And no man shall come up with thee, neither let any man be seen throughout all the mount; neither let the flocks nor herds feed before that mount
the mount kept clear for the meeting
Moses alone with God, the sole mediator (a figure of Christ)
the holy mount kept clear in reverence (Exodus 19:12-13)

schools.summary: Moses obeying and ascending with the tables: 'and he hewed two tables of stone like unto the first; and Moses rose up early in the morning, and went up unto mount Sinai, as the LORD had commanded him, and took in his hand the two tables of stone' — Moses obeying, hewing the two tables, rising early, and going up Sinai with the tables, as commanded (vv.1-2, the obedience, Moses doing all as God commanded, the ascent, Moses going up with the new tables, the promptness, rising early, the ready obedience); Moses hewing the tables (preparing the stone for God's writing) — the obedience (the new tables); 'Moses rose up early in the morning, and went up unto mount Sinai, as the LORD had commanded him' (Moses' prompt obedience, rising early, ascending with the tables, the providence, Moses going up to receive the renewed law and the proclamation of the Name, vv.5-7) — Gill.

themes: And he hewed two tables of stone like unto the first; and Moses rose up early in the morning, and went up unto mount Sinai, as the LORD had commanded him
Moses obeying and ascending with the tables
Moses promptly obeying, doing all as commanded (vv.1-2)
ascending with the new tables to meet God (vv.5-7)

schools.summary: The LORD descending and proclaiming his Name: 'and the LORD descended in the cloud, and stood with him there, and proclaimed the name of the LORD' — the LORD descending in the cloud (the visible token of his presence) and standing with Moses, and proclaiming his Name (the descent, God coming down to meet Moses, as he had promised, Exodus 33:19-23, the standing with him, God's gracious nearness, the proclamation, God himself proclaiming his Name, vv.6-7); the LORD descending in the cloud (the manifestation of his presence, the shekinah) — the gracious coming (God fulfilling his promise to show his glory, Exodus 33:19-23); 'proclaimed the name of the LORD' (God proclaiming his Name, his character, his attributes, vv.6-7, the revelation, God himself declaring who he is, his name a revelation of his nature, the providence, the great proclamation, the fullest revelation of God's character, vv.6-7, the answer to Moses' prayer, 'shew me thy glory,' Exodus 33:18) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD descended in the cloud, and stood with him there, and proclaimed the name of the LORD
the LORD descending and proclaiming his Name
God coming down to reveal his character (Exodus 33:19-23)
the answer to Moses' prayer for his glory (Exodus 33:18)

schools.summary: The proclamation of the divine Name: 'and the LORD passed by before him, and proclaimed, The LORD, The LORD God, merciful and gracious, longsuffering, and abundant in goodness and truth' — the LORD passing before Moses (as promised, Exodus 33:22) and proclaiming his Name, declaring his character (the proclamation, the fullest revelation of God's nature, the Name unfolded, the passing by, the glory passing before Moses in the cleft of the rock); the great revelation — 'The LORD, The LORD God, merciful and gracious, longsuffering, and abundant in goodness and truth': the proclamation of God's character, the fullest revelation of his attributes in the Old Testament (the witnesses expounding the names — 'The LORD, The LORD' (Jehovah, Jehovah, the self-existent, eternal, faithful God, the covenant name doubled for emphasis); 'God' (El, the mighty); 'merciful' (compassionate, tender, full of pity); 'gracious' (favorable, kind, giving unmerited grace); 'longsuffering' (slow to anger, patient, bearing long with sinners); 'abundant in goodness and truth' (full of mercy and faithfulness, rich in steadfast love and reliability); the lesson — the revelation of God as the merciful, gracious, patient, faithful God, the ground of all hope for sinners, this the most-quoted self-revelation of God in Scripture, echoed, Numbers 14:18; Nehemiah 9:17; Psalm 86:15; 103:8; 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2, 'a gracious God, and merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness,' the character of God the foundation of the gospel, the mercy and grace fully revealed in Christ); the merciful, gracious, longsuffering, abundant God — the revelation of God's grace and faithfulness (the providence, God revealing his character to Moses and to all, the ground of hope, the mercy that would pardon the apostate people, vv.9) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What is the significance of the proclamation of the Name?

sides.position: The fullest revelation of God's character in the Old Testament - the merciful, gracious, patient, faithful God, the most-quoted self-revelation of God in Scripture (Numbers 14:18; Psalm 86:15; 103:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2), the ground of all hope for sinners, fully revealed in Christ

themes: And the LORD passed by before him, and proclaimed, The LORD, The LORD God, merciful and gracious, longsuffering, and abundant in goodness and truth
the proclamation of the divine Name (the crux of the revelation of God's character)
the fullest revelation of God's nature, the most-quoted self-revelation (Numbers 14:18; Psalm 86:15; 103:8)
the merciful and gracious God, the ground of hope, fully revealed in Christ (Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2)

schools.summary: God's mercy and justice in the proclamation: 'keeping mercy for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, and that will by no means clear the guilty; visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children, and upon the children's children, unto the third and to the fourth generation' — God keeping mercy for thousands (of generations, or persons), forgiving every kind of sin (iniquity, transgression, sin, the abounding mercy, the vast, generous forgiveness, the mercy for thousands far outweighing the visiting to the third and fourth generation, Exodus 20:5-6, the forgiveness, the pardon of all manner of sin); the forgiving of all manner of sin (iniquity, transgression, sin, the three words covering every kind) — the full forgiveness (God's readiness to pardon, the ground of hope, the mercy magnified); the justice — 'that will by no means clear the guilty': God, though merciful, by no means clearing (acquitting, leaving unpunished) the guilty (the impenitent, the obstinate, the witnesses noting the lesson — the holding together of mercy and justice, God merciful, yet just, pardoning the penitent, yet not clearing the guilty, his justice not set aside by his mercy, the great question — how God can be both 'merciful... forgiving iniquity' and yet 'by no means clear the guilty' — answered fully in the cross of Christ, where mercy and justice meet, Romans 3:25-26, 'that he might be just, and the justifier of him which believeth in Jesus,' the guilt punished in Christ, the believer pardoned, God just and the justifier, 'visiting the iniquity of the fathers' — the justice that visits sin, the consequences of sin descending, the warning, Exodus 20:5); 'visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... unto the third and to the fourth generation' (the visiting of sin, the consequences, the justice, Exodus 20:5, the providence, the mercy and the justice both revealed, the mercy abounding, the justice sure, the full character of God, fulfilled in the cross) — Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: How are God's mercy and 'by no means clearing the guilty' reconciled?

sides.position: The holding together of mercy and justice - the abounding forgiveness (mercy for thousands) and the sure justice (not clearing the guilty, Exodus 20:5-6); the great tension answered in the cross where God is 'just, and the justifier of him which believeth in Jesus' (Romans 3:25-26), the guilt punished in Christ, the believer pardoned

themes: Keeping mercy for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, and that will by no means clear the guilty; visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... unto the third and to the fourth generation
God's mercy and justice in the proclamation
the abounding mercy (for thousands) and the sure justice (not clearing the guilty, Exodus 20:5-6)
mercy and justice met and reconciled in the cross, God just and the justifier (Romans 3:25-26)

schools.summary: Moses worshipping at the proclamation: 'and Moses made haste, and bowed his head toward the earth, and worshipped' — Moses, hearing the proclamation of God's character (vv.6-7), making haste to bow and worship (the worship, the reverent, immediate response to the revelation of God, the haste, the prompt, eager worship, the bowing, the humble adoration before the merciful and just God); Moses' haste to worship (the immediate, reverent response) — the awe (the revelation of God's glory and character moving Moses to instant worship, the humility, bowing to the earth); 'and worshipped' (Moses worshipping, adoring the merciful, gracious, just God, the figure, the right response to the revelation of God, worship and adoration, the providence, Moses worshipping, then pleading, vv.9, the revelation of God moving to worship) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And Moses made haste, and bowed his head toward the earth, and worshipped
Moses worshipping at the proclamation
the reverent, immediate worship at the revelation of God's glory and character (vv.6-7)
the right response to the revelation of God, worship and adoration

schools.summary: Moses pleading God's presence and pardon: 'and he said, If now I have found grace in thy sight, O Lord, let my Lord, I pray thee, go among us; for it is a stiffnecked people; and pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for thine inheritance' — Moses, encouraged by the proclamation of mercy, pleading that God would go among the people (his presence, Exodus 33:14-16), pardon their iniquity, and take them for his inheritance (the plea, Moses interceding, pressing the promise of mercy, the presence, Moses pleading for God's presence to go with them); Moses pleading 'it is a stiffnecked people' — the remarkable plea (Moses turning the very reason for wrath, vv.32:9, into a reason for God's presence and pardon — the people so stubborn that they need God's presence the more, the argument from the people's need, the worse they are, the more they need God); 'pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for thine inheritance' (Moses pleading for pardon, the forgiveness of the calf, and for God to claim them as his inheritance, his own people, the covenant restored, the providence, Moses' intercession, pressing the mercy proclaimed, vv.6-7, the covenant renewed, vv.10) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he said, If now I have found grace in thy sight, O Lord, let my Lord, I pray thee, go among us; for it is a stiffnecked people; and pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for thine inheritance
Moses pleading God's presence and pardon
Moses pressing the mercy proclaimed, pleading God's presence (Exodus 33:14-16)
turning the people's stubbornness into a reason for God's presence (Exodus 32:9)

schools.summary: The renewal of the covenant and the promise of marvels: 'and he said, Behold, I make a covenant: before all thy people I will do marvels, such as have not been done in all the earth, nor in any nation: and all the people among which thou art shall see the work of the LORD: for it is a terrible thing that I will do with thee' — God renewing the covenant (after the breach of the calf), promising to do marvels (wonders) before the people, unmatched in all the earth (the renewal, the covenant restored by grace, the marvels, the wonders God would work, the driving out of the nations, the bringing into the land, vv.11, the displays of his power); God making (renewing) the covenant (the broken covenant restored, of God's free grace) — the grace (the covenant renewed despite the people's sin, the pure mercy of God, the figure of the new covenant in Christ, the covenant of grace); 'all the people... shall see the work of the LORD: for it is a terrible thing that I will do with thee' (the work of the LORD, the marvels, the wonders, terrible, awesome, the providence, the covenant renewed, the marvels to follow, the conquest of Canaan, the displays of God's power, God's grace and power restoring the covenant) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And he said, Behold, I make a covenant: before all thy people I will do marvels, such as have not been done in all the earth... and all the people among which thou art shall see the work of the LORD
the renewal of the covenant and the promise of marvels
the broken covenant renewed of free grace (a figure of the covenant of grace in Christ)
the marvels of God's power promised, the conquest of Canaan (vv.11)

schools.summary: The promise to drive out the nations: 'observe thou that which I command thee this day: behold, I drive out before thee the Amorite, and the Canaanite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Hivite, and the Jebusite' — God promising to drive out the nations of Canaan before Israel (the six nations named), if Israel would observe his commands (the promise, the conquest of the land, the nations driven out, vv.10, the condition, the observing of God's commands, the obedience required, the providence, the land given, the nations driven out by God's power); the command to observe (to keep God's commands, the condition of the blessing) — the obedience (the covenant requiring obedience, the law to be kept); 'I drive out before thee the Amorite, and the Canaanite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Hivite, and the Jebusite' (the nations of Canaan driven out, by God's power, the conquest, the providence, the land given, the nations driven out, the warning against their idolatry, vv.12-17) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Observe thou that which I command thee this day: behold, I drive out before thee the Amorite, and the Canaanite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Hivite, and the Jebusite
the promise to drive out the nations
the land given by God's power, the nations driven out (vv.10)
the obedience required as the condition of the blessing

schools.summary: The warning against covenant with the nations: 'take heed to thyself, lest thou make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land whither thou goest, lest it be for a snare in the midst of thee' — Israel warned not to make a covenant (a league, alliance) with the inhabitants of Canaan, lest it become a snare (a trap, leading to idolatry, the warning, against alliance with the idolatrous nations, the snare, the danger of being drawn into their idolatry); the prohibition of covenant with the nations (no league with the idolaters) — the separation (Israel to be separate from the idolatrous nations); 'lest it be for a snare in the midst of thee' (the snare, the danger of being drawn into idolatry by the alliance, the figure, the danger of unholy alliances, the believer not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers, 2 Corinthians 6:14-17, the snare of worldly association, the providence, the warning against the snare of the nations' idolatry, vv.13-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Take heed to thyself, lest thou make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land whither thou goest, lest it be for a snare in the midst of thee
the warning against covenant with the nations
the danger of alliance with the idolatrous nations, leading to idolatry
the believer not to be unequally yoked (2 Corinthians 6:14-17)

schools.summary: The command to destroy the idols: 'but ye shall destroy their altars, break their images, and cut down their groves' — Israel to destroy the altars, break the images (pillars, statues), and cut down the groves (Asherah, the sacred poles or trees) of the nations' idolatry (the destruction, the utter removal of the idolatrous worship, no compromise, the thoroughness, the altars, images, groves all destroyed); the destroying of the altars and images (the instruments of idolatry) — the thoroughness (the idolatry rooted out, Deuteronomy 7:5; 12:3, the no compromise, the idols utterly destroyed); 'and cut down their groves' (the groves, the Asherah, the sacred poles or trees of idolatry, cut down, the figure, the utter rooting out of idolatry, the figure of the believer's destroying of every idol and false worship, the providence, the command to destroy idolatry, no toleration, the holiness of God's people) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But ye shall destroy their altars, break their images, and cut down their groves
the command to destroy the idols
the utter removal of idolatry, no compromise (Deuteronomy 7:5; 12:3)
the figure of the believer's destroying of every idol and false worship

schools.summary: The jealous God who claims exclusive worship: 'for thou shalt worship no other god: for the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God' — Israel to worship no other god, for the LORD, whose name is 'Jealous,' is a jealous God (the exclusivity, the worship of God alone, no other, the first commandment, Exodus 20:3-5, the jealousy, God's jealous claim to exclusive worship and love); the name — 'whose name is Jealous': God's name is Jealous (one of his names, expressing his nature, the witnesses noting the meaning — God's jealousy, not the sinful, petty jealousy of men, but the holy jealousy of God, who will not share his glory and his people's worship and love with another, the jealousy of love, as a husband for his wife, God claiming the exclusive love and worship of his people, Exodus 20:5; Deuteronomy 4:24, the figure of the marriage covenant, God the husband of his people, jealous for their faithfulness, Hosea 2; James 4:5, 'the spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to envy,' or yearns jealously; 2 Corinthians 11:2, 'I am jealous over you with godly jealousy,' the lesson — God's exclusive claim, his jealousy for his own honor and his people's love, the wrong of idolatry as spiritual adultery, the unfaithfulness of giving to another what belongs to God alone); God a jealous God — the holy jealousy (the exclusive claim to worship and love, the providence, the jealousy of God the ground of the warning against idolatry, the exclusive devotion required) — Ellicott, Gill.

sides.position: Not the sinful jealousy of men, but the holy jealousy of love that will not share his people's worship and love with another (Exodus 20:5; Deuteronomy 4:24) - the exclusive claim of the divine Husband for his people's faithfulness (Hosea 2; 2 Corinthians 11:2), the wrong of idolatry as spiritual adultery

themes: For thou shalt worship no other god: for the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God
the jealous God who claims exclusive worship (the crux of God's jealousy)
God's holy jealousy of love, the exclusive claim to his people's worship (Exodus 20:5; Deuteronomy 4:24)
the divine Husband jealous for his people's faithfulness, idolatry as spiritual adultery (Hosea 2; 2 Corinthians 11:2)

schools.summary: The snare of the nations' idolatrous feasts: 'lest thou make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land, and they go a whoring after their gods, and do sacrifice unto their gods, and one call thee, and thou eat of his sacrifice' — the warning repeated (vv.12): no covenant with the nations, lest they (going a whoring after their gods, the idolatry) draw Israel into their idolatrous feasts (inviting them to eat of the idol sacrifices, the snare, the idolatrous feasts, the invitation to partake, the going a whoring, idolatry as spiritual fornication, Hosea 1-2); the 'go a whoring after their gods' (idolatry as spiritual fornication, unfaithfulness to God) — the figure (idolatry as adultery, the unfaithfulness to the divine Husband, vv.14); 'one call thee, and thou eat of his sacrifice' (the danger of partaking in the idol feasts, the invitation to eat of the sacrifices, the participation in idolatry, 1 Corinthians 10:20-21, the figure, the danger of fellowship with idols, the believer not to partake of the table of devils, the providence, the snare of the idolatrous feasts, the warning against participation) — Gill.

themes: Lest thou make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land, and they go a whoring after their gods... and one call thee, and thou eat of his sacrifice
the snare of the nations' idolatrous feasts
idolatry as spiritual fornication, unfaithfulness to God (Hosea 1-2)
the danger of partaking in the idol feasts (1 Corinthians 10:20-21)

schools.summary: The warning against intermarriage with idolaters: 'and thou take of their daughters unto thy sons, and their daughters go a whoring after their gods, and make thy sons go a whoring after their gods' — the warning against intermarriage with the idolatrous nations: taking their daughters for Israel's sons would lead the daughters (and through them the sons) into idolatry (the danger, the idolatrous wives drawing the sons into idolatry, as later, 1 Kings 11:4, Solomon, the snare, the intermarriage leading to apostasy); the prohibition of intermarriage with idolaters (the daughters of the nations not to be taken) — the danger (the unequal yoke, the idolatrous influence, Deuteronomy 7:3-4); 'their daughters go a whoring after their gods, and make thy sons go a whoring after their gods' (the idolatrous wives leading the sons into idolatry, the apostasy, 1 Kings 11:4, the figure, the danger of unequal marriage, the believer not to be unequally yoked, 2 Corinthians 6:14, the providence, the snare of intermarriage with idolaters, the warning against apostasy) — Gill.

themes: And thou take of their daughters unto thy sons, and their daughters go a whoring after their gods, and make thy sons go a whoring after their gods
the warning against intermarriage with idolaters
the idolatrous wives drawing the sons into idolatry (Deuteronomy 7:3-4; 1 Kings 11:4)
the believer not to be unequally yoked (2 Corinthians 6:14)

schools.summary: The prohibition of molten gods: 'thou shalt make thee no molten gods' — Israel forbidden to make molten (cast metal) gods, as the golden calf (vv.32:4, the prohibition, the second commandment, no images, Exodus 20:4, the warning, the recent sin of the calf, the molten god, freshly condemned); the prohibition of molten gods (the images, the cast idols, the second commandment, Exodus 20:4-5) — the reminder (the calf, the molten god lately made, vv.32:4, the no images, the worship of God not by images); no molten gods — the warning against the recent sin (the calf, the figure, the worship of God in spirit and truth, not by images, John 4:24, the providence, the prohibition of idolatry, the renewed covenant requiring pure worship) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: Thou shalt make thee no molten gods
the prohibition of molten gods
the second commandment, no images (Exodus 20:4; 32:4)
the worship of God in spirit and truth, not by images (John 4:24)

schools.summary: The renewed command of the feast of unleavened bread: 'the feast of unleavened bread shalt thou keep. Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread, as I commanded thee, in the time of the month Abib: for in the month Abib thou camest out from Egypt' — the renewed covenant including the keeping of the feast of unleavened bread (the Passover feast, seven days, in Abib, the month of the Exodus, Exodus 12:15-20; 13:3-7, the feast, the memorial of the deliverance from Egypt, the renewal, the feasts re-enjoined in the renewed covenant); the seven days' unleavened bread (the memorial of the haste of the deliverance, Exodus 12:15) — the figure (the feast of sincerity and truth, the unleavened bread, 1 Corinthians 5:7-8, 'Christ our passover is sacrificed for us... let us keep the feast... with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth'); 'for in the month Abib thou camest out from Egypt' (the time of the deliverance, Abib, the month of the Exodus, the memorial, the feast commemorating the redemption, the providence, the feasts of the renewed covenant, the memorial of the redemption, the figure of Christ our Passover) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Gill.

themes: The feast of unleavened bread shalt thou keep. Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread... for in the month Abib thou camest out from Egypt
the renewed command of the feast of unleavened bread
the memorial of the deliverance from Egypt (Exodus 12:15-20)
the figure of Christ our Passover and the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Corinthians 5:7-8)

schools.summary: The firstborn claimed by God: 'all that openeth the matrix is mine; and every firstling among thy cattle, whether ox or sheep, that is male' — God claiming all the firstborn (that openeth the womb), of man and beast, as his own (the claim, the firstborn the LORD's, by the sparing in Egypt, Exodus 13:2, 12-15, the firstlings, the firstborn of the cattle, the males, the LORD's); the firstborn claimed (the LORD's, by the sparing of Israel's firstborn in Egypt, Exodus 13:15) — the claim (the firstborn consecrated to God, the figure, the firstborn the LORD's, the figure of Christ the firstborn, Colossians 1:15, 18, and the church of the firstborn, Hebrews 12:23); 'every firstling among thy cattle, whether ox or sheep, that is male' (the firstborn of the cattle, the males, the LORD's, the providence, the firstborn consecrated, the figure of the first and best given to God, the firstborn redeemed, vv.20) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: All that openeth the matrix is mine; and every firstling among thy cattle, whether ox or sheep, that is male
the firstborn claimed by God
the firstborn the LORD's, by the sparing in Egypt (Exodus 13:2, 12-15)
the figure of Christ the firstborn (Colossians 1:15, 18; Hebrews 12:23)

schools.summary: The redemption of the firstborn: 'but the firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb: and if thou redeem him not, then shalt thou break his neck. All the firstborn of thy sons thou shalt redeem. And none shall appear before me empty' — the firstborn of the ass (an unclean beast, not for sacrifice) to be redeemed with a lamb, or its neck broken, and all the firstborn sons redeemed (the redemption, the firstborn bought back, Exodus 13:13, the lamb the redemption price for the ass); the ass (unclean) redeemed by a lamb (or its neck broken) — the figure (the unclean redeemed by the lamb, or destroyed, the figure of redemption by the Lamb, Christ, the soul redeemed by the Lamb, or perishing, 1 Peter 1:18-19); 'All the firstborn of thy sons thou shalt redeem. And none shall appear before me empty' (the firstborn sons redeemed, Exodus 13:13, 15, the redemption price, Numbers 18:15-16, 'none shall appear before me empty,' none to come before God without an offering, the gift required in worship, Deuteronomy 16:16-17, the providence, the firstborn redeemed by the lamb, the figure of redemption by Christ, the offering required in coming to God) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But the firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb... All the firstborn of thy sons thou shalt redeem. And none shall appear before me empty
the redemption of the firstborn
the firstborn redeemed by the lamb (Exodus 13:13), the figure of redemption by Christ the Lamb (1 Peter 1:18-19)
none to appear before God empty, the offering required (Deuteronomy 16:16-17)

schools.summary: The renewed command of the Sabbath rest: 'six days thou shalt work, but on the seventh day thou shalt rest: in earing time and in harvest thou shalt rest' — the renewed covenant including the Sabbath rest (six days' work, the seventh a rest), even in the busiest seasons (plowing time and harvest, the Sabbath, the weekly rest, the fourth commandment, Exodus 20:8-11, the renewal, the Sabbath re-enjoined in the renewed covenant); the seventh-day rest (the weekly Sabbath) — the figure (the rest of God, the figure of the rest in Christ, the gospel rest, Hebrews 4:9-10, 'there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God'); 'in earing time and in harvest thou shalt rest' (the Sabbath kept even in plowing and harvest, the busiest times, when the temptation to work is greatest, the lesson, the Sabbath to be kept even when business presses, the rest not set aside for gain, the trust in God's provision, the providence, the Sabbath sacred even in the busy seasons, the figure of the rest in God) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Six days thou shalt work, but on the seventh day thou shalt rest: in earing time and in harvest thou shalt rest
the renewed command of the Sabbath rest
the weekly rest kept even in the busiest seasons (Exodus 20:8-11)
the figure of the rest in Christ (Hebrews 4:9-10)

schools.summary: The feasts of weeks and ingathering: 'and thou shalt observe the feast of weeks, of the firstfruits of wheat harvest, and the feast of ingathering at the year's end' — the renewed covenant including the feast of weeks (Pentecost, the firstfruits of the wheat harvest, Leviticus 23:15-21) and the feast of ingathering (Tabernacles, at the year's end, the harvest gathered, Leviticus 23:33-43, the feasts, the three annual feasts, the firstfruits and the ingathering, the renewal, the feasts re-enjoined); the feast of weeks (Pentecost, the firstfruits) — the figure (the firstfruits, the figure of Christ the firstfruits, 1 Corinthians 15:20, 23, and the firstfruits of the Spirit, Pentecost, Acts 2, the church the firstfruits, James 1:18); 'the feast of ingathering at the year's end' (the feast of ingathering, Tabernacles, the harvest home, the gathering of the fruits, the figure, the final ingathering, the harvest of the world, the gathering of the redeemed, Matthew 13:30, 39; Revelation 14:15, the providence, the three feasts, the firstfruits and the ingathering, figures of Christ and the harvest of redemption) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt observe the feast of weeks, of the firstfruits of wheat harvest, and the feast of ingathering at the year's end
the feasts of weeks and ingathering
the firstfruits (Pentecost) a figure of Christ the firstfruits and the Spirit (1 Corinthians 15:20; Acts 2)
the ingathering (Tabernacles) a figure of the harvest of the redeemed (Matthew 13:30)

schools.summary: The three yearly appearances before God: 'thrice in the year shall all your men children appear before the Lord GOD, the God of Israel' — all the males to appear before God three times a year (at the three feasts: unleavened bread/Passover, weeks/Pentecost, ingathering/Tabernacles, the three appearances, the men gathering before God at the central sanctuary, the worship, the assembling of the people before God thrice yearly, Deuteronomy 16:16); the three yearly appearances (the men gathering before God) — the worship (the assembling of the people); 'before the Lord GOD, the God of Israel' (appearing before God, the men presenting themselves at the sanctuary, the figure, the assembling of God's people for worship, the figure of the gathering of the church, Hebrews 10:25, the providence, the three feasts, the gathering before God, the worship of the covenant people) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: Thrice in the year shall all your men children appear before the Lord GOD, the God of Israel
the three yearly appearances before God
the men gathering before God at the three feasts (Deuteronomy 16:16)
the figure of the gathering of the church for worship (Hebrews 10:25)

schools.summary: God's promise of protection during the feasts: 'for I will cast out the nations before thee, and enlarge thy borders: neither shall any man desire thy land, when thou shalt go up to appear before the LORD thy God thrice in the year' — God promising to cast out the nations, enlarge Israel's borders, and protect their land so that no enemy would desire (covet, invade) it while the men went up to the feasts (the promise, the protection of the land during the feasts, the providence, God guarding their homes while they worshipped, the reward of obedience); the casting out of the nations and the enlarging of the borders (the conquest, the blessing) — the promise (the providence); 'neither shall any man desire thy land, when thou shalt go up... thrice in the year' (the remarkable promise, the land protected, no enemy invading, while the men were away at the feasts, the figure, God guarding what is committed to him, the trust rewarded, the seeking first the kingdom and all things added, Matthew 6:33, the providence, the protection of the land, the reward of putting God's worship first) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: For I will cast out the nations before thee, and enlarge thy borders: neither shall any man desire thy land, when thou shalt go up to appear before the LORD thy God thrice in the year
God's promise of protection during the feasts
God guarding the land while the men worship, the reward of obedience
the reward of seeking first the kingdom (Matthew 6:33)

schools.summary: The law of the Passover sacrifice: 'thou shalt not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leaven; neither shall the sacrifice of the feast of the passover be left unto the morning' — the blood of the sacrifice not to be offered with leaven (the leaven, the figure of sin and corruption, not with the blood), and the Passover sacrifice not left till morning (eaten the same night, Exodus 12:10, the laws, the purity of the sacrifice, no leaven, the Passover wholly eaten, the figures, the unleavened sacrifice, the figure of the sinless Christ, the Passover not left, the urgency); the prohibition of leaven with the blood (the leaven of sin not with the sacrifice) — the figure (the sinless sacrifice, Christ without leaven, the unleavened Lamb, 1 Corinthians 5:7-8); 'neither shall the sacrifice of the feast of the passover be left unto the morning' (the Passover eaten the same night, nothing left, Exodus 12:10, the figure, the Passover wholly partaken, the urgency, the haste of the deliverance, the providence, the laws of the Passover, the figure of Christ our Passover, sinless and wholly given) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leaven; neither shall the sacrifice of the feast of the passover be left unto the morning
the law of the Passover sacrifice
the unleavened sacrifice, the figure of the sinless Christ (1 Corinthians 5:7-8)
the Passover wholly eaten, nothing left (Exodus 12:10)

schools.summary: The firstfruits and the law of the kid: 'the first of the firstfruits of thy land thou shalt bring unto the house of the LORD thy God. Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk' — the firstfruits of the land to be brought to God's house, and Israel not to boil a kid in its mother's milk (the firstfruits, the first and best given to God, Proverbs 3:9, the law of the kid, a prohibition, perhaps against a pagan rite, or a law of mercy, not to mingle the source of life with death); the firstfruits brought to God's house (the first and best, Proverbs 3:9) — the figure (the firstfruits, the figure of Christ the firstfruits, and the first and best given to God); 'thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk' (the prohibition, variously explained: against a Canaanite idolatrous rite, or a law of mercy and natural fitness, not to mingle the milk that nourished life with the death of the kid, the figure, the law of mercy, the tenderness, or the separation from pagan rites, the providence, the law's purity and mercy, the separation from the nations' practices) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB.

themes: The first of the firstfruits of thy land thou shalt bring unto the house of the LORD thy God. Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk
the firstfruits and the law of the kid
the first and best given to God (Proverbs 3:9)
the law of mercy and separation from pagan rites

schools.summary: The command to write the covenant words: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Write thou these words: for after the tenor of these words I have made a covenant with thee and with Israel' — God commanding Moses to write these words (the laws of vv.10-26, the terms of the renewed covenant), for upon these words the covenant with Israel was made (the writing, Moses recording the covenant terms, the covenant, the renewed covenant, made upon these words); Moses writing the words (the covenant terms recorded, the inspired Scripture) — the written covenant (the record); 'after the tenor of these words I have made a covenant with thee and with Israel' (the covenant made on these terms, the renewed covenant, the conditions, vv.10-26, the providence, the covenant renewed and recorded, the broken covenant restored, the words written, the law of the renewed covenant) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Write thou these words: for after the tenor of these words I have made a covenant with thee and with Israel
the command to write the covenant words
Moses recording the terms of the renewed covenant (vv.10-26)
the broken covenant restored and written

schools.summary: Moses' forty days' fast and the writing of the ten commandments: 'and he was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights; he did neither eat bread, nor drink water. And he wrote upon the tables the words of the covenant, the ten commandments' — Moses again forty days and nights with the LORD, fasting (neither eating nor drinking, sustained by God, the forty days, the long communion, the second time, Deuteronomy 9:18, the fast, the miraculous abstinence, sustained by God's presence, the parallel of Moses, Elijah, and Christ, 1 Kings 19:8; Matthew 4:2); the forty days' fast (the miraculous sustenance, the communion with God) — the figure (the man of God sustained by God's presence, the bread of God, the communion above natural need); 'he wrote upon the tables the words of the covenant, the ten commandments' (the Ten Commandments written on the tables, the 'he' debated: God writing, as vv.1; Exodus 34:1, 'I will write,' and Deuteronomy 10:4, God wrote, or Moses writing at God's dictation, but most hold God wrote the ten words, as the first tables, Exodus 32:16, the ten commandments, the moral law, the heart of the covenant, written again, the providence, the law restored, the Ten Commandments written, the renewed covenant) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And he was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights; he did neither eat bread, nor drink water. And he wrote upon the tables the words of the covenant, the ten commandments
Moses' forty days' fast and the writing of the ten commandments
the long communion, the miraculous fast (Deuteronomy 9:18; cf. Elijah, Christ, 1 Kings 19:8; Matthew 4:2)
the moral law restored, the Ten Commandments written, the covenant renewed (Exodus 34:1; 32:16)

schools.summary: The shining of Moses' face: 'and it came to pass, when Moses came down from mount Sinai with the two tables of testimony in Moses' hand, when he came down from the mount, that Moses wist not that the skin of his face shone while he talked with him' — the wonder — 'the skin of his face shone while he talked with him': Moses, coming down with the tables, not knowing that the skin of his face shone (radiated, gave off rays of light) from his communion with God (the witnesses noting the meaning — the reflected glory of God, Moses' face shining from being in God's presence, the glory caught from beholding God, 2 Corinthians 3:7, 'the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance,' the lesson — the glory of communion with God, the face shining, reflecting the glory beheld, as the believer beholding the glory of the Lord is changed into the same image, 2 Corinthians 3:18, 'we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory,' the glory of the ministry of the law, the old covenant ministry glorious, though to be exceeded by the gospel, 2 Corinthians 3:7-11, the unconscious shining, Moses unaware, the humility, the grace not self-conscious, the figure of the believer transformed, unaware of his own shining); Moses knew not his face shone (the humility, the unconscious glory) — the figure (the believer changed, unaware, the grace shining without self-consciousness, the reflected, not self-originated, glory); the face shining from communion with God — the reflected glory (the glory of beholding God, the providence, the glory of the ministry, the figure of the transforming glory of communion, 2 Corinthians 3:18) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the shining of Moses' face signify?

sides.position: The reflected glory of God, caught from beholding him (2 Corinthians 3:7) - the glory of communion that transforms (2 Corinthians 3:18, the believer changed from glory to glory), the glory of the old covenant ministry (glorious, though exceeded by the gospel, 2 Corinthians 3:7-11); the unconscious shining, the humility of grace

themes: And it came to pass, when Moses came down from mount Sinai... that Moses wist not that the skin of his face shone while he talked with him
the shining of Moses' face (the crux of the glory of the ministry)
the reflected glory of God, caught from beholding him (2 Corinthians 3:7)
the transforming glory of communion, the believer changed from glory to glory (2 Corinthians 3:18)

schools.summary: The people afraid of the shining face: 'and when Aaron and all the children of Israel saw Moses, behold, the skin of his face shone; and they were afraid to come nigh him' — Aaron and the people, seeing Moses' shining face, afraid to come near him (the fear, the people overawed by the glory, afraid to approach, the glory, the reflected glory of God on Moses' face, too bright, too awful for the people); the shining face (the reflected glory, 2 Corinthians 3:7) — the glory of the ministry of the law (the glory that the people could not stedfastly behold); 'they were afraid to come nigh him' (the people's fear, overawed, afraid to approach the glory, the figure, the glory of the law terrifying, the people kept at a distance, as at Sinai, Exodus 20:18-19, the ministration of the law that condemns, before which sinners fear, 2 Corinthians 3:7-9, the providence, the glory of Moses' face, the figure of the glory of the law, awful to the people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And when Aaron and all the children of Israel saw Moses, behold, the skin of his face shone; and they were afraid to come nigh him
the people afraid of the shining face
the people overawed by the reflected glory (2 Corinthians 3:7)
the figure of the glory of the law before which sinners fear (Exodus 20:18-19; 2 Corinthians 3:7-9)

schools.summary: Moses calling the rulers back: 'and Moses called unto them; and Aaron and all the rulers of the congregation returned unto him: and Moses talked with them' — Moses calling the fearful people, and Aaron and the rulers returning to him, and he talking with them (delivering God's words, the call, Moses reassuring them, calling them back, the return, the rulers coming near, though the people had feared, the speaking, Moses delivering the covenant words); Moses calling the people back (reassuring them, not leaving them at a distance) — the grace (the mediator calling the fearful near); 'Aaron and all the rulers of the congregation returned unto him: and Moses talked with them' (the rulers returning, drawing near at Moses' call, the speaking, Moses delivering God's words, vv.32, the providence, the mediator calling the people near, delivering the covenant, the contrast with the veil, vv.33) — Gill.

themes: And Moses called unto them; and Aaron and all the rulers of the congregation returned unto him: and Moses talked with them
Moses calling the rulers back
the mediator calling the fearful near, reassuring them
delivering the covenant words (vv.32)

schools.summary: Moses delivering all God's commands: 'and afterward all the children of Israel came nigh: and he gave them in commandment all that the LORD had spoken with him in mount Sinai' — all the people coming near, and Moses giving them in commandment all that the LORD had spoken on the mount (the renewed covenant, the laws, the delivery, Moses faithfully declaring all God's words, the coming near, the people, reassured, drawing near to hear, the commandment, the covenant terms delivered); the people coming near (reassured, drawing near to hear) — the access (the people brought near by the mediator); 'he gave them in commandment all that the LORD had spoken with him in mount Sinai' (Moses delivering all God's words, the faithful mediator, declaring all the counsel of God, Acts 20:27, the covenant, the renewed covenant delivered to the people, the providence, the mediator faithfully delivering God's word, the covenant given) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And afterward all the children of Israel came nigh: and he gave them in commandment all that the LORD had spoken with him in mount Sinai
Moses delivering all God's commands
Moses faithfully declaring all the counsel of God (Acts 20:27)
the renewed covenant delivered to the people

schools.summary: The veil on Moses' face: 'and till Moses had done speaking with them, he put a vail on his face' — Moses, after speaking to the people, putting a veil over his shining face (vv.34-35, removing it when he went in before the LORD, putting it on again when he came out to the people, the witnesses noting the meaning — the veil hiding the glory, the lesson, expounded fully by Paul, 2 Corinthians 3:13-16 — Moses 'put a vail over his face, that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished,' the fading glory of the law, the veil a figure of the obscurity of the old covenant, the glory of the law veiled, not fully seen, the fading, transient glory hidden, and the veil on the heart of Israel, 2 Corinthians 3:14-15, 'their minds were blinded: for until this day remaineth the same vail untaken away in the reading of the old testament... when Moses is read, the vail is upon their heart,' the veil that remains over the hearts of those who read the old covenant without seeing Christ, the lesson — the veil done away in Christ, 2 Corinthians 3:14, 'which vail is done away in Christ,' and 'when it shall turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away,' 3:16, the contrast with the gospel, the unveiled face beholding the glory, 2 Corinthians 3:18, the fading glory of the law veiled, the abiding glory of the gospel open); Moses veiling the glory (hiding the shining, the fading glory not to be gazed on) — the figure (the obscurity of the law, the glory veiled, the fading splendor, the contrast with the open glory of the gospel); the veil — the figure of the veiled, fading glory of the old covenant, and the veil on the heart removed in Christ (2 Corinthians 3:13-16, the providence, the veil, the figure of the obscurity of the law, done away in Christ) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the veil on Moses' face signify?

sides.position: The figure of the obscurity and fading glory of the old covenant (2 Corinthians 3:13), and the veil on the hearts of those who read the law without seeing Christ (3:14-15) - the veil done away in Christ and taken away when the heart turns to the Lord (3:14, 16), the contrast with the unveiled glory of the gospel (3:18)

themes: And till Moses had done speaking with them, he put a vail on his face
the veil on Moses' face (the crux of the veiled glory)
the figure of the obscurity and fading glory of the old covenant (2 Corinthians 3:13)
the veil on the heart removed in Christ, the contrast with the open glory of the gospel (2 Corinthians 3:14-16, 18)

schools.summary: Moses removing the veil before the LORD: 'but when Moses went in before the LORD to speak with him, he took the vail off, until he came out. And he came out, and spake unto the children of Israel that which he was commanded' — Moses removing the veil when he went in before the LORD (unveiled in God's presence), and speaking to the people what he was commanded (the unveiling before God, Moses with open face before the LORD, the figure, the believer with unveiled face before God, beholding his glory, 2 Corinthians 3:18, the open access to God in Christ); Moses unveiled before the LORD (the open face in God's presence) — the figure (the unveiled access to God, 2 Corinthians 3:18, 'with open face,' the believer beholding the glory of the Lord unveiled); 'he came out, and spake unto the children of Israel that which he was commanded' (Moses delivering God's word, then veiling again, vv.35, the providence, the unveiled communion with God, the veiled speaking to the people, the figure of the open glory in Christ, the veiled glory of the law) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: But when Moses went in before the LORD to speak with him, he took the vail off, until he came out. And he came out, and spake unto the children of Israel that which he was commanded
Moses removing the veil before the LORD
Moses unveiled in God's presence, the open face before the LORD
the figure of the open access and beholding of glory in Christ (2 Corinthians 3:18)

schools.summary: Moses veiling again before the people: 'and the children of Israel saw the face of Moses, that the skin of Moses' face shone: and Moses put the vail upon his face again, until he went in to speak with him' — the people seeing Moses' shining face (as he came from God), and Moses putting the veil on again until he went back in before the LORD (the cycle, unveiled before God, veiled before the people, the glory, the shining face seen, then veiled, the figure, the veiled glory before the people, the open glory before God); the shining face (the reflected glory, seen by the people) — the glory of the ministry (2 Corinthians 3:7); 'Moses put the vail upon his face again, until he went in to speak with him' (the veil on again before the people, the glory hidden, removed again before God, the figure, the veiled, fading glory of the law before the people, the open, abiding glory before God and in Christ, 2 Corinthians 3:13-18, the providence, the cycle of veiling and unveiling, the figure of the old covenant's veiled glory and the new covenant's open glory, the veil done away in Christ) — Gill.

themes: And the children of Israel saw the face of Moses, that the skin of Moses' face shone: and Moses put the vail upon his face again, until he went in to speak with him
Moses veiling again before the people
the cycle - veiled before the people, unveiled before God
the veiled, fading glory of the law and the open glory of the gospel (2 Corinthians 3:13-18)
